title: Building a Productive Balcony Garden in Singapore
body:
Singapore's tropical climate is both a gift and a challenge for balcony gardeners. The year-round warmth means growing seasons don't exist in the traditional sense — you can plant almost anything, almost any time. But the intense sun, humidity, and frequent afternoon storms require a more thoughtful approach than temperate-climate gardening guides typically suggest.

Start with the right containers

For a balcony setup, weight is a real constraint. Fabric grow bags have become my default — they're lightweight, encourage healthy root development through air pruning, and drain extremely well, which matters enormously in a climate where overwatering is the most common mistake.

I run three sizes: small 3-gallon bags for herbs (Thai basil, laksa leaf, Vietnamese coriander), medium 7-gallon bags for chillies and compact tomato varieties, and two large 15-gallon bags where I'm currently growing eggplant and a dwarf papaya.

The best garden is the one you can actually maintain consistently — not the most ambitious one you'll abandon in three months.

Soil and nutrition

Commercial potting mixes sold locally are often too dense and water-retentive for tropical container growing. I blend my own: two parts good quality potting mix, one part perlite, and a generous handful of slow-release fertiliser. The perlite dramatically improves drainage and air circulation around roots.
